content: Can you describe the main types of tasks you do in your job?
Research, teaching and organising seminars and network events.
In your research, teaching, or organising seminars and network events, where do you actually use generative AI today—can you walk me through one recent example of how it fit into the workflow?

I have, together with my son, made an app which relies on an API for OpenAI Chat GPT, which allows students to react to a Youtube-video, record discussions and respond with written answers to questions and receiving AI-feedback which they finally react to.
When students use the app and get the AI-feedback, what have you noticed it changes in their discussions or written answers compared with when they don’t get that feedback?
This is to early to tell. Only a very restricted pilot use has yet been done.

summary:
## Rolling interview summary (so far)

### Role / main tasks
- Interviewee’s job involves **research, teaching, and organizing seminars and network events**.

### GenAI actual usage & perceived usefulness (current focus)
- **Direct GenAI use case:** Interviewee (together with their son) **built an app** that uses the **OpenAI ChatGPT API**.
  - **Purpose / workflow:** Students use the app to:
    - **React to a YouTube video**
    - **Record discussions**
    - **Write answers to questions**
    - Receive **AI-generated feedback**
    - Then **respond/react to the AI feedback** (i.e., iterative reflection).
- **Observed impact so far:** Interviewee says it is **too early to tell** what changes the AI feedback produces in students’ discussions or written answers.
  - Reason: only a **very limited pilot** has been conducted.

### Evidence, examples, and boundaries
- No concrete examples yet of:
  - Clear productivity/learning gains
  - Failures or problematic outputs
  - Situations where they deliberately avoid GenAI

### Emotions / sensitivity flags
- No explicit emotional reactions (positive or negative) expressed yet; tone appears **cautious/uncertain** due to early-stage pilot results.
content: When you and your son built and piloted the ChatGPT-based app, what parts of getting it working and using it in practice felt straightforward, and what parts were harder than you expected?

summary:
## Rolling interview summary (updated)

### Role / main tasks
- Interviewee’s job involves **research, teaching, and organizing seminars and network events**.

### GenAI actual usage & perceived usefulness
- **Direct GenAI use case:** Interviewee (with their son) **built an app** using the **OpenAI ChatGPT API**.
  - **Student workflow:** Students **react to a YouTube video**, **record discussions**, **write answers to questions**, receive **AI-generated feedback**, then **respond to the AI feedback** (iterative reflection).
- **Observed impact so far:** Interviewee says it is **too early to tell** whether AI feedback changes students’ discussions or written answers due to a **very limited pilot**.

### Perceived ease of use & integration (Prompt 2)
- **Harder than expected / friction points:**
  - **API payment/billing management** was “a bit tricky,” mainly to avoid the risk of **over-use** (cost control).
  - **Layout and programming** described as a **“never-ending back-and-forth”** process (iterative development effort).
- **What they set up to feel safe using it:**
  - Implemented a **password function** (access control) to reduce risk of uncontrolled usage.
  - After setup, they found the **cost risk was small** because **price per token is very low** and **transparent**; ultimately “**no problem**.”

### Evidence, examples, and boundaries
- Still **no concrete examples** yet of:
  - Clear productivity/learning gains
  - Failures or harmful/problematic outputs
  - Situations where they deliberately avoid GenAI

### Emotions / sensitivity flags
- Tone remains **pragmatic and cautious** (early pilot stage), with mild initial concern about **cost/overuse risk** that was **resolved** after implementing controls. No strong positive/negative emotional reactions reported.
content: When you were building the app and setting things up—like adding the password and watching the token costs—what kinds of feelings did you notice in yourself as you worked with the ChatGPT part?

summary:
## Rolling interview summary (updated)

### Role / main tasks
- Interviewee’s work involves **research, teaching, and organizing seminars and network events**.

### GenAI actual usage & perceived usefulness
- **Direct GenAI use case:** Interviewee (with their son) **built an app** using the **OpenAI ChatGPT API**.
  - **Student workflow:** Students **react to a YouTube video**, **record discussions**, **write answers to questions**, receive **AI-generated feedback**, then **respond to the AI feedback** (iterative reflection).
- **Observed impact so far:** Interviewee says it is **too early to tell** whether AI feedback changes students’ discussions or written answers due to a **very limited pilot**.
- **Design intent / boundary:** Their focus is to **“engage the human brain, not substitute cognitive efforts.”** They position the tool as supporting reflection rather than replacing thinking.

### Perceived ease of use & integration (Prompt 2)
- **Harder than expected / friction points:**
  - **API payment/billing management** was “a bit tricky,” mainly to avoid the risk of **over-use** (cost control).
  - **Layout and programming** described as a **“never-ending back-and-forth”** process (iterative development effort).
- **What they set up to feel safe using it:**
  - Implemented a **password function** (access control) to reduce risk of uncontrolled usage.
  - After setup, they found the **cost risk was small** because **price per token is very low** and **transparent**; ultimately “**no problem**.”

### Emotional drivers and feelings around GenAI (Prompt 3)
- **Low emotional intensity reported:** Building and configuring the ChatGPT component (including password and token-cost monitoring) **“did not trigger such kinds of feelings.”**
- **No role-threat feelings:** They report that **at no point** did they feel AI **challenged their human role** as “managers of our own work.”
- Overall stance remains **pragmatic and cautious**, with emphasis on **human control and cognitive engagement** rather than substitution.

### Evidence, examples, and boundaries (so far)
- Still **no concrete examples** yet of:
  - Clear productivity/learning gains
  - Failures, harmful/problematic outputs, or “crossing the line” moments
  - Situations where they deliberately avoid GenAI in other work tasks

### Sensitivity / flags for future interviewers
- No expressed anxiety, stress, or discomfort; mild earlier concern about **cost/overuse** was addressed via controls.
- Strong value statement to probe later: **AI should not substitute cognitive effort**; could be explored with specific pilot moments or hypothetical edge cases.
content: As you’ve been using the app to give students AI feedback, what have you noticed about how consistent and trustworthy the feedback feels from one student or session to the next?

summary:
## Rolling interview summary (updated)

### Role / main tasks
- Interviewee’s work involves **research, teaching, and organizing seminars and network events**.

### GenAI actual usage & perceived usefulness
- **Direct GenAI use case:** Interviewee (with their son) **built an app** using the **OpenAI ChatGPT API**.
  - **Student workflow:** Students **react to a YouTube video**, **record discussions**, **write answers to questions**, receive **AI-generated feedback**, then **respond to the AI feedback** (iterative reflection).
- **Design intent / boundary:** Tool is meant to **“engage the human brain, not substitute cognitive efforts.”** Positioned as supporting reflection rather than replacing thinking.
- **Observed impact so far:** **Too early to tell** whether AI feedback changes students’ discussions or written answers due to a **very limited pilot**.

### Perceived ease of use & integration (Prompt 2)
- **Harder than expected / friction points:**
  - **API payment/billing management** was “a bit tricky,” mainly to avoid **over-use** (cost control).
  - **Layout and programming** described as a **“never-ending back-and-forth”** (iterative development effort).
- **Controls to make it feel safe/usable:**
  - Implemented a **password function** (access control) to reduce risk of uncontrolled usage.
  - After setup, they found the **cost risk was small** because **price per token is low and transparent**; ultimately “**no problem**.”

### Emotional drivers and feelings around GenAI (Prompt 3)
- **Low emotional intensity:** Building/configuring the ChatGPT component (password + token-cost monitoring) **“did not trigger such kinds of feelings.”**
- **No role-threat feelings:** They did not feel AI challenged their human role as “managers of our own work.”
- Overall stance: **pragmatic and cautious**, emphasizing **human control** and **cognitive engagement**.

### Perceived technical characteristics: reliability, consistency, trust (Prompt 5)
- When asked about **consistency/trustworthiness of AI feedback across students/sessions**, interviewee said it is **too early to tell**.
- **Quality check used so far:** They **tested the app several times with realistic exchanges** before sharing/using it with students (no further criteria described).
- No concrete examples yet of **problematic outputs**, **inconsistency**, or **accuracy failures**.

### Evidence, examples, and boundaries (so far)
- Still **no concrete examples** of:
  - Clear productivity/learning gains
  - Failures, harmful/problematic outputs, or “crossing the line” moments
  - Situations where they deliberately avoid GenAI in other work tasks

### Sensitivity / flags for future interviewers
- Interviewee **ended the interview due to time constraints** (“I have no more time…”).
- No expressed anxiety, stress, or discomfort about GenAI itself; earlier mild concern about **cost/overuse** was addressed via controls.
- Value statement to probe later: **AI should not substitute cognitive effort** (ask for specific edge cases or thresholds).
content: When you were testing the app with those realistic exchanges, did the AI ever come across to you as having a particular “personality” or way of thinking that shaped the feedback?

### Emotional drivers and feelings around GenAI (Prompt 3)
- **Low emotional intensity:** Building/configuring the ChatGPT component (password + token-cost monitoring) **“did not trigger such kinds of feelings.”**
- **No role-threat feelings:** They did not feel AI challenged their human role as “managers of our own work.”
- Overall stance: **pragmatic and cautious**, emphasizing **human control** and **cognitive engagement**.

### Perceived technical characteristics: reliability, consistency, trust (Prompt 5)
- When asked about **consistency/trustworthiness of AI feedback across students/sessions**, interviewee said it is **too early to tell**.
- **Quality check used so far:** They **tested the app several times with realistic exchanges** before sharing/using it with students (no further criteria described).
- No concrete examples yet of **problematic outputs**, **inconsistency**, or **accuracy failures**.

### Evidence, examples, and boundaries (so far)
- Still **no concrete examples** of:
  - Clear productivity/learning gains
  - Failures, harmful/problematic outputs, or “crossing the line” moments
  - Situations where they deliberately avoid GenAI in other work tasks

### Sensitivity / flags for future interviewers
- Interviewee **ended the interview due to time constraints** (“I have no more time…”).
- No expressed anxiety, stress, or discomfort about GenAI itself; earlier mild concern about **cost/overuse** was addressed via controls.
- Value statement to probe later: **AI should not substitute cognitive effort** (ask for specific edge cases or thresholds).
content: Before you go—when you say “no,” do you mainly see it as just a programmed tool, or do you think it has any tendencies or “defaults” that shape what kind of feedback it gives?

## Rolling interview summary (updated)

### Role / main tasks
- Interviewee’s work involves **research, teaching, and organizing seminars and network events**.

### GenAI actual usage & perceived usefulness (Prompt 1)
- **Direct GenAI use case:** Interviewee (with their son) **built an app** using the **OpenAI ChatGPT API**.
  - **Student workflow:** Students **react to a YouTube video**, **record discussions**, **write answers to questions**, receive **AI-generated feedback**, then **respond to the AI feedback** (iterative reflection).
- **Design intent / boundary:** Tool is meant to **“engage the human brain, not substitute cognitive efforts.”** Positioned as supporting reflection rather than replacing thinking.
- **Observed impact so far:** **Too early to tell** whether AI feedback changes students’ discussions or written answers due to a **very limited pilot**.
- Still **no examples provided** of other day-to-day GenAI uses, deliberate non-use, or clear productivity/learning gains.

### Perceived ease of use & integration (Prompt 2)
- **Harder than expected / friction points:**
  - **API payment/billing management** was “a bit tricky,” mainly to avoid **over-use** (cost control).
  - **Layout and programming** described as a **“never-ending back-and-forth”** (iterative development effort).
- **Controls to make it feel safe/usable:**
  - Implemented a **password function** (access control) to reduce risk of uncontrolled usage.
  - After setup, they found the **cost risk was small** because **price per token is low and transparent**; ultimately “**no problem**.”

### Emotional drivers and feelings around GenAI (Prompt 3)
- **Low emotional intensity:** Building/configuring the ChatGPT component (password + token-cost monitoring) **“did not trigger such kinds of feelings.”**
- **No role-threat feelings:** They did not feel AI challenged their human role as “managers of our own work.”
- Overall stance: **pragmatic and cautious**, emphasizing **human control** and **cognitive engagement**.

### Perceived technical characteristics: reliability, consistency, trust (Prompt 5)
- When asked about **consistency/trustworthiness of AI feedback across students/sessions**, interviewee said it is **too early to tell**.
- **Quality check used so far:** They **tested the app several times with realistic exchanges** before sharing/using it with students (no further criteria described).
- No concrete examples yet of **problematic outputs**, **inconsistency**, or **accuracy failures**.

### Perceived human-likeness and agency (Prompt 6)
- When asked whether the AI came across as having a **“personality” or way of thinking**, interviewee answered **“No”**.
- Interviewee **did not elaborate** on whether they see it as purely programmed vs. having tendencies/defaults, due to **time constraints** and ending the interview.

### Adoption barriers, risks, and future intentions (Prompt 7) — limited data so far
- Main practical concern mentioned: **avoiding over-use/cost control**, addressed via **passwording and monitoring**.
- No expressed concerns so far about broader organizational risks, ethics/legal issues, or job displacement.

### Sensitivity / flags for future interviewers
- Interview ended abruptly due to **time pressure** (“No, and bye for now!” / “I have no time…”).
- No signs of anxiety about GenAI itself; the only mild concern previously was **cost/overuse**, which they felt was manageable.
- Value statement to probe later: **AI should not substitute cognitive effort**—ask for concrete thresholds/edge cases and where they would *not* want AI involved.
content: Thinking about the app you built and the “human brain first” boundary you mentioned, what would have to be in place for you to feel comfortable scaling it up or using GenAI more broadly in your teaching and seminar work?
